response:
THE LAW OF MOORE, WHICH STATES THAT THE NUMBER OF TRANSISTORS ON A MICROPROCESSOR DOUBLES EVERY TWO YEARS, HAS BEEN A DRIVING FORCE IN TECHNOLOGICAL PROGRESS FOR DECADES. HOWEVER, AS WE APPROACH THE PHYSICAL LIMITS OF SILICON BASED TECHNOLOGY, IT IS BECOMING CLEAR THAT THE LAW WILL EVENTUALLY CEASE TO HOLD TRUE. THIS IS DUE TO THE FACT THAT WE ARE REACHING THE MINIMUM SIZE AT WHICH TRANSISTORS CAN BE MANUFACTURED, MAKING FURTHER MINIATURIZATION IMPOSSIBLE WITHOUT SIGNIFICANT INNOVATIONS IN MATERIALS AND DESIGN.

*** 

THE FUTURE OF COMPUTING MAY LIE IN ALTERNATIVE MATERIALS SUCH AS GRAPHENE OR QUANTUM COMPUTING, WHICH CAN OFFER SIGNIFICANT PERFORMANCE GAINS WITHOUT RELYING ON THE SAME SCALING PRINCIPLES. WHILE IT IS DIFFICULT TO PREDICT EXACTLY WHEN THE LAW OF MOORE WILL CEASE TO BE VALID, IT IS CLEAR THAT THE TECHNOLOGY INDUSTRY WILL NEED TO ADAPT TO NEW REALITIES IN ORDER TO CONTINUE ADVANCING. THIS TRANSITION WILL HAVE WIDE-RANGING IMPACTS ON INDUSTRIES SUCH AS DIGITAL ADVERTISING, WHERE COMPUTING POWER AND EFFICIENCY ARE CRITICAL FACTORS IN SUCCESS.
